name: Etteilla II
description:
# Etteilla II — Grand Livre de Thot

> Part of the Etteilla lineage — hub at `10-etteilla.mdx`; see also `10a` and `10c`.

## At a glance

The **second-generation** Etteilla: a posthumous re-edition of Alliette's system,
published **c.1838–1840** (often associated with the publisher **Simon Blocquel** and the
likely-pseudonymous **"Julia Orsini"**). It carries Etteilla's divinatory framework
forward with **altered titling and imagery** — a brand continued by his "school," not by
his hand.

## Origin & dating

Issued roughly **1838 (or 1840)**, decades after Alliette's death (1791). Marketed under
the *Grand Livre de Thot* title and the name **Julia Orsini** — believed to be a publisher's
pseudonym rather than a real cartomancer. Cards were **hand-water-coloured**.

## Provenance & evidence

Surviving decks and accompanying booklets; catalogued in collector and reconstruction
literature. It is a **variant** of Etteilla I — same divinatory DNA, different drawings,
some renamed cards and revised keywords.

## Game or divination?

**Divination** — squarely within the cartomantic tradition Etteilla founded; never a game
deck.

## The fear question

By the 1830s–40s, cartomancy in France was a **commercial publishing genre** — printed,
titled, sold openly with instruction booklets. The "fear" register here is essentially
zero for the publisher: fortune-telling decks were a normal product of the Paris print
trade. (The criminalised, precarious end of the practice remained the *itinerant* reader,
not the boxed retail deck.)

## Counter-voices

The proliferation of Etteilla editions shows how quickly a **manufactured tradition**
("ancient Egyptian tarot") becomes a self-sustaining market. That is not evidence of
suppressed antiquity; it is evidence of a **successful 19th-century commercial myth**.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Confirm the 1838 vs. 1840 dating and the Blocquel/Orsini attribution in the grammar.
- Catalogue exactly which cards differ from Etteilla I (titles, numbers, imagery) rather
  than assuming identity.

## Sources & further reading

- "Etteilla," Wikipedia; Tarot Heritage and benebell wen Etteilla reconstructions;
  Druidcraft blog, "The Etteilla Tarot (Type I/II/III)."

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13) — metadata correction.** The per-card `metadata.symbols` on the minor (pip) cards had been templated from **Rider-Waite-Smith (1909)** scene iconography (e.g. a 'sunflower'/'cat' on the Queen, 'bandage'/'infinity' on pips) — anachronistic for Etteilla's 1788 near-pip pack, whose minors are arranged suit-symbols with printed keyword titles, not Smith's narrative scenes. Those false symbol lists have been REMOVED. The cards' divinatory sections (Etteilla's own upright/reversed meanings) are unaffected. The `archetype: card:<rank>-of-<suit>` fields are kept only as structural cross-references (King of Bâtons ↔ King of Wands), not as a claim of shared imagery. See research/cards/<this deck>.md.

etymology.notes: Represents the male querent (significator). In Hermetic philosophy, this is the Ein Sof or Tao—divine consciousness before manifestation.
etymology.origin: From Greek 'χάος' (khaos) meaning the primordial void or abyss

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Corresponds to the Ein Sof in Kabbalah, the Tao in Eastern philosophy, and the prima materia in alchemy

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Chaos — the male querent (significator)
sections.Upright: The male consultant himself: the matter as it now stands, full of unformed potential. The fertile void before the question takes shape.
sections.Reversed: A question asked from confusion or anxiety; the void felt as emptiness rather than promise.
sections.Etymology: Chaos / The Void. From Greek 'χάος' (khaos) meaning the primordial void or abyss. Represents the male querent (significator). In Hermetic philosophy, this is the Ein Sof or Tao—divine consciousness before manifestation.
sections.Historical Context:
In Etteilla's system, Le Chaos represents the first moment of the Genesis creation narrative—the primordial void before 'Let there be light.' This card draws heavily from the Hermetic text 'Poimandres' (Divine Pymander), where creation begins with an infinite darkness containing all potential.

Jean-Baptiste Alliette (Etteilla) believed tarot originated with the Egyptian priests of the Temple of Ptah at Memphis, encoding ancient wisdom about cosmic origins. The Chaos card embodies this philosophy: it is not emptiness but pregnant potential, the divine spirit before it takes form.

In practical cartomancy, this card serves as the male querent's significator—representing the questioner himself when male. Etteilla's 1783 'Manière de se récréer avec le jeu de cartes nommées tarots' established this convention, which persists in Grand Etteilla readings today.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Corresponds to the Ein Sof in Kabbalah, the Tao in Eastern philosophy, and the prima materia in alchemy
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **image redrawn.** The pre-creation void is redrawn **without the sun** — logical, since in the cosmogony the sun is not yet created. Full title now printed both sides [@web_steve_grandet]. 

*Sources:* [@web_etteilla_org], [@web_steve_grandet].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md).
sections.Scene: Le Chaos in the Egyptian Etteilla shows the male Questioner emerging from swirling primordial forms rendered in the Egyptomanie style — perhaps with hieroglyph-like borders or pyramidal forms in the background. The Egyptian version of the Chaos card situates the questioner within the context of ancient Egyptian cosmogony, the undifferentiated void before the first act of creation.
sections.Symbol: The Male Questioner in Egyptian Chaos — the self at the threshold of an ancient oracle, the human being who initiates the reading within the world of the pharaohs.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:3 'Let there be light' - the first act of creation
etymology.origin: From Latin 'lumen' meaning light, radiance, or understanding

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The Divine Nous (Mind) of Hermetic philosophy; the first emanation of the Absolute

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Lumière — Light / Clarification
sections.Upright: Illumination and clarity — understanding dawning on the matter (Etteilla's 'éclaircissement').
sections.Reversed: Fire and ardour turned destructive; clarity flaring into heat and haste.
sections.Etymology: The Light / Enlightenment. From Latin 'lumen' meaning light, radiance, or understanding. Corresponds to Genesis 1:3 'Let there be light' - the first act of creation
sections.Historical Context:
La Lumière represents the first divine act in Genesis: 'And God said, Let there be light: and there was light.' In Etteilla's Hermetic interpretation, this is not merely physical illumination but the emergence of consciousness and understanding from the void.

The card draws from the Corpus Hermeticum, where light represents divine Nous (Mind) becoming aware of itself. Etteilla's system uniquely places this as card 2, immediately following Chaos, establishing a creation sequence that differs markedly from the Tarot de Marseille tradition.

In the 1843 'Jeu de la princesses' version, this card appears as 'Osiris or the Sun,' connecting to Egyptian solar theology.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The Divine Nous (Mind) of Hermetic philosophy; the first emanation of the Absolute
sections.Scene: La Lumière in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts the first light of creation in an Egyptian idiom — perhaps as the solar disc of Ra rising, or as a divine emanation from the eye of Horus. The Egyptian iconographic vocabulary transforms the Genesis narrative into the cosmogony of the Nile civilization, light as divine solar power rather than the Judeo-Christian first day.
sections.Symbol: The First Light in Egyptian form — the solar disc rising, creation's first act rendered in the language of the Nile civilization.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:11-12, the creation of vegetation on the third day
etymology.origin: From Latin 'planta' meaning plant, sprout, or cutting

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The vegetable soul; the life force animating organic matter

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Les Plantes — Growth / Discourse
sections.Upright: What is proposed takes root; growth, talk that bears fruit, the green beginning of a thing.
sections.Reversed: Things still fluid and unsettled; words that run off like water, nothing yet rooted.
sections.Etymology: The Plants / Vegetation. From Latin 'planta' meaning plant, sprout, or cutting. Corresponds to Genesis 1:11-12, the creation of vegetation on the third day
sections.Historical Context:
Les Plantes embodies the third day of Genesis creation, when God commanded the earth to bring forth grass, herbs, and fruit-bearing trees. In Etteilla's system, this represents the first manifestation of organic life—the transition from abstract elements to living, growing things.

The Hermetic interpretation connects this card to the vegetable soul in Neoplatonic philosophy—the animating principle that distinguishes living matter from mere elements. Etteilla saw in vegetation the first evidence of divine life force manifesting in material form.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The vegetable soul; the life force animating organic matter
sections.Scene: Les Plantes in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts vegetation and growth in an Egyptian or Near Eastern style — perhaps with lotus flowers, papyrus reeds, or Egyptian botanical motifs. The plant life of creation is shown through the symbolic plants of the Nile rather than European botanical forms.
sections.Symbol: Plants in Egyptian idiom — lotus and papyrus as the vegetation of creation, growth rendered through the symbolic flora of the Nile civilization.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:6-8, the creation of the firmament
etymology.origin: From Latin 'caelum' meaning sky, heavens, or celestial vault

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The celestial sphere; the realm of planetary intelligences and fixed stars

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Ciel — The Heavens
sections.Upright: Favour from above: expansion, protection, a matter blessed and widening.
sections.Reversed: A blessing withheld or delayed; distance, a sky that does not answer.
sections.Etymology: The Heavens / The Sky. From Latin 'caelum' meaning sky, heavens, or celestial vault. Corresponds to Genesis 1:6-8, the creation of the firmament
sections.Historical Context:
Le Ciel represents the creation of the firmament—the great dome separating the waters above from the waters below. In ancient cosmology, this was understood as the physical structure of heaven itself, the boundary between earthly and divine realms.

In Hermetic philosophy, the celestial sphere contains the planetary intelligences that govern earthly affairs. Etteilla integrated astrological correspondences throughout his system, seeing the heavens as a living influence rather than mere backdrop.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The celestial sphere; the realm of planetary intelligences and fixed stars
sections.Scene: Le Ciel in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts the celestial vault in an Egyptian cosmographic style — perhaps with the figure of Nut (the sky goddess) arching over the horizon, or with an Egyptian celestial map. The heavens are shown as they were conceived in Egyptian religious thought rather than in European astronomical terms.
sections.Symbol: The Heavens in Egyptian form — perhaps Nut arching as the sky goddess, the celestial vault rendered in the cosmographic language of ancient Egypt.

items.name: L'Homme et les Quadrupèdes (Man and Quadrupeds)

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:24-27, creation of land animals and humanity
etymology.literal: Man and the Quadrupeds / Humanity and Animals

metadata.genesis_day: Day 6 - Creation of Animals and Humanity
metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The microcosm; humanity as reflection of cosmic order

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: L'Homme et les Quadrupèdes — Man and Beasts
sections.Upright: The living world of work and livelihood; natural strength, sustenance, the body's labour.
sections.Reversed: The brute in man; appetite unchecked, toil without reward.
sections.Etymology: Man and the Quadrupeds / Humanity and Animals. From Latin 'homo' (man) and 'quadrupes' (four-footed animal). Corresponds to Genesis 1:24-27, creation of land animals and humanity
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 6 - Creation of Animals and Humanity
sections.Historical Context:
This card depicts the sixth day of creation, when God made the beasts of the earth and then humanity in His own image. In Etteilla's system, this represents the culmination of material creation—the emergence of conscious beings capable of reflecting the divine.

The Hermetic tradition sees humanity as the microcosm—containing within itself all the elements and principles of the greater cosmos. Etteilla's card reflects this: the human figure stands as the meeting point of heaven and earth, spirit and matter.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The microcosm; humanity as reflection of cosmic order
sections.Scene: L'Homme et les Quadrupèdes in the Egyptian Etteilla shows man and beast in an Egyptian context — perhaps with Egyptian animal-headed deities (Anubis, Horus) or with scenes from Egyptian daily life showing the human relationship to domesticated or sacred animals. The card reflects the Egyptian religious tradition's profound intertwining of human and animal forms.
sections.Symbol: Man and animals in Egyptian form — perhaps the jackal of Anubis or the sacred bull of Apis, the human-animal relationship expressed through Egyptian divine iconography.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:14-19, creation of sun, moon, and stars
etymology.origin: From Latin 'stella' meaning star or celestial body

metadata.genesis_day: Day 4 - Creation of Sun, Moon, and Stars
metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The stellar intelligences; astrological influences on human affairs

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Les Astres — The Stars
sections.Upright: The stars favourable: hope, guidance, a destiny inclined to the good.
sections.Reversed: An ill or clouded star; hope deferred, a guiding light obscured.
sections.Etymology: The Stars. From Latin 'stella' meaning star or celestial body. Corresponds to Genesis 1:14-19, creation of sun, moon, and stars
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 4 - Creation of Sun, Moon, and Stars
sections.Historical Context:
Les Étoiles depicts the fourth day of Genesis, when God created the sun, moon, and stars to govern day and night and to mark seasons, days, and years. For Etteilla, this was not merely astronomical but deeply astrological—the stars influence human destiny.

Etteilla's 'Société des Interprètes du Livre de Thot' systematically integrated astrology with tarot. The stars card embodies this synthesis: celestial bodies are not distant objects but active powers shaping earthly events.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The stellar intelligences; astrological influences on human affairs
sections.Correction: Correction (2026-06-13): name/french_name reconciled to 'Les Astres' (day-4 luminaries trump).
sections.Scene: Les Astres in the Egyptian Etteilla shows the celestial bodies in an Egyptian astronomical context — the sun disc of Ra, the crescent of Thoth, and the star-system of the Egyptian sky chart. The celestial bodies are personified in Egyptian divine form, the cosmic order made visible through the mythology of the Egyptian pantheon.
sections.Symbol: The Celestial Bodies in Egyptian form — Ra, Thoth, and the stars of the Egyptian sky, cosmic order expressed through the mythology of the Nile.

items.name: Les Oiseaux et les Poissons (Birds and Fishes)

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 1:20-23, creation of sea and sky creatures on day five

metadata.genesis_day: Day 5 - Creation of Birds and Fish
metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The elemental realms of Air and Water; mobility between states

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Les Oiseaux et les Poissons — Birds and Fishes
sections.Upright: Air and water teeming: news, messages, plenty, journeys, fertile movement.
sections.Reversed: Idle gossip, scattered news, a voyage or message delayed.
sections.Etymology: The Birds and the Fish. From Latin 'avis' (bird) and 'piscis' (fish). Corresponds to Genesis 1:20-23, creation of sea and sky creatures on day five
sections.Genesis Connection: Day 5 - Creation of Birds and Fish
sections.Historical Context:
Les Oiseaux et les Poissons represents the fifth day of creation, when God filled the waters with fish and the sky with birds. These are the first animals—creatures that inhabit the fluid realms of water and air rather than solid earth.

In Etteilla's Hermetic symbolism, birds and fish represent mobility and freedom in their respective elements. Birds ascend toward the divine; fish explore the depths of the unconscious.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The elemental realms of Air and Water; mobility between states
sections.Scene: Les Oiseaux et les Poissons in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts birds and fish in an Egyptian register — perhaps the ibis of Thoth, the falcon of Horus, and the sacred fish of the Nile. The animated realms of air and water are shown through the sacred animals of Egyptian religious iconography.
sections.Symbol: Birds and fish in Egyptian form — the ibis and the falcon, the sacred fish of the Nile, the animated realms rendered through Egyptian sacred zoology.

etymology.notes: Corresponds to Genesis 2:2-3, the seventh day when God rested and made it holy
etymology.origin: From Latin 'repausare' meaning to rest, pause, or cease from labor

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The Sabbath; sacred rest; return to unity

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Repos — Rest
sections.Upright: The seventh day: rest, completion, peace earned after labour.
sections.Reversed: Idleness and stagnation; rest curdled into inertia.
sections.Etymology: Rest / Repose. From Latin 'repausare' meaning to rest, pause, or cease from labor. Corresponds to Genesis 2:2-3, the seventh day when God rested and made it holy
sections.Historical Context:
Le Repos represents the Sabbath—the seventh day when God rested from creation and sanctified rest itself. In Etteilla's system, this card serves as the female querent's significator, balancing Le Chaos which represents male questioners.

The 1865 Etteilla III booklet states: 'The true sense of this card is profound peace.' This is not mere inactivity but sacred completion—the rest that comes after work is finished.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The Sabbath; sacred rest; return to unity
sections.Scene: Le Repos in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts rest after creation in an Egyptian idiom — perhaps a reclining figure beneath the eye of Ra, or a scene of divine completion with Egyptian symbolic elements. The Sabbath rest is reframed as the completion of an Egyptian creation cycle.
sections.Symbol: Sacred rest in Egyptian form — the divine pause after creation rendered through the symbolic vocabulary of the Nile civilization.

etymology.notes: One of the four cardinal virtues from Plato's Republic
etymology.origin: From Latin 'justitia' meaning righteousness, equity, or lawfulness

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Ma'at; cosmic balance; the weighing of souls

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Justice — Justice
sections.Upright: Lawful judgment, balance restored, a matter set right.
sections.Reversed: Injustice, bias, the law bent or misused.
sections.Etymology: Justice. From Latin 'justitia' meaning righteousness, equity, or lawfulness. One of the four cardinal virtues from Plato's Republic
sections.Historical Context:
Following the seven days of creation, Etteilla's trumps transition to the four cardinal virtues as described by Plato: Justice, Temperance, Fortitude, and Prudence. La Justice represents the first of these—the principle of righteous judgment and fair dealing.

In the Hermetic tradition, Justice connects to Ma'at—the Egyptian goddess of truth, balance, and cosmic order.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Ma'at; cosmic balance; the weighing of souls
sections.Scene: La Justice in the Egyptian Etteilla is depicted with Egyptian symbolic elements — perhaps with the scales of Ma'at (Egyptian goddess of truth and justice) replacing or augmenting the classical sword-and-scales iconography. The weighing of the heart in the Egyptian afterlife tradition makes this an especially resonant card in the Egyptomanie idiom.
sections.Symbol: Justice with Ma'at's scales — the Egyptian goddess of truth and measure informing the classical personification, justice as cosmic principle in the language of the afterlife weighing.

etymology.notes: The second cardinal virtue; the golden mean between excess and deficiency
etymology.origin: From Latin 'temperantia' meaning self-control, moderation, or sobriety

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: The alchemical mixing of elements; the art of proportion

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Tempérance — Temperance
sections.Upright: Moderation and prudent management; opposites blended well.
sections.Reversed: Excess and imbalance; the measure lost.
sections.Etymology: Temperance / Moderation. From Latin 'temperantia' meaning self-control, moderation, or sobriety. The second cardinal virtue; the golden mean between excess and deficiency
sections.Historical Context:
La Tempérance represents the second Platonic virtue—the principle of moderation and self-control that enables harmonious living. The traditional image shows a figure pouring liquid between vessels, representing the careful mixing and balancing of elements.

This alchemical symbolism was not lost on Etteilla, who saw in temperance the secret of transformation.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: The alchemical mixing of elements; the art of proportion
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **image redrawn.** The figure no longer pours liquid between two vessels; instead she holds an **elephant's bridle ("the Bridle of Restraint")** with an **elephant beside her** — a 17th-century animal correspondence for Temperance imported into the redraw [@web_steve_grandet; @web_benebell_etteilla]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_etteilla], [@web_steve_grandet].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md).
sections.Scene: La Tempérance in the Egyptian Etteilla shows the virtue of moderation in an Egyptian visual context — perhaps with Egyptian ceramic vessels or Nilotic imagery replacing the classical two-vessel pouring scene. The Egyptian version frames temperance within a civilization renowned for its measured hydraulic engineering of the Nile.
sections.Symbol: Temperance in Egyptian form — the virtue of measured pouring rendered through Egyptian vessel and Nilotic imagery.

etymology.notes: The third cardinal virtue; moral courage rather than mere physical power
etymology.origin: From Latin 'fortitudo' meaning courage, bravery, or strength of character

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Mastery of the animal nature; courage guided by wisdom

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Force — Strength
sections.Upright: Courage and self-mastery; force rightly governed.
sections.Reversed: Weakness, or strength misused as domination.
sections.Etymology: Strength / Fortitude. From Latin 'fortitudo' meaning courage, bravery, or strength of character. The third cardinal virtue; moral courage rather than mere physical power
sections.Historical Context:
La Force embodies the third Platonic virtue—fortitude or courage. This is not mere physical strength but moral courage: the capacity to face difficulty, danger, or pain without yielding to fear.

The traditional image often shows a figure with a lion, representing the mastery of animal instincts through gentle strength rather than violent domination.
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Mastery of the animal nature; courage guided by wisdom
sections.Scene: La Force in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts strength in an Egyptian idiom — perhaps as Sekhmet the lion-headed goddess, or as a pharaoh subduing enemies beneath his feet. The Egyptian version transforms the classical virtue of fortitude into the specific imagery of divine solar power and royal mastery.
sections.Symbol: Strength in Egyptian form — perhaps Sekhmet or the pharaoh's lion-mastery, the virtue of fortitude expressed through Egyptian divine power.

etymology.notes: The fourth cardinal virtue; considered by Aristotle as 'practical wisdom'
etymology.origin: From Latin 'prudentia' meaning foresight, wisdom, or practical judgment

metadata.hermetic_correspondence: Phronesis; practical wisdom; the inner counselor

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Prudence — Prudence
sections.Upright: Foresight and careful counsel (Etteilla set Prudence where ordinary tarot has the Hanged Man).
sections.Reversed: Imprudence and rashness; a clear warning ignored.
sections.Etymology: Prudence / Wisdom. From Latin 'prudentia' meaning foresight, wisdom, or practical judgment. The fourth cardinal virtue; considered by Aristotle as 'practical wisdom'
sections.Historical Context:
La Prudence completes the quartet of cardinal virtues—representing practical wisdom, foresight, and good judgment. Aristotle called this 'phronesis,' the ability to discern the right course of action in particular circumstances.

The traditional image often shows a figure with a mirror (self-knowledge) and a serpent (wisdom).
sections.Hermetic Correspondence: Phronesis; practical wisdom; the inner counselor
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **image redrawn.** Instead of a figure stepping around a serpent on the ground, the snake now **climbs her arm**, and she holds a **mirror** in that hand (self-knowledge + wisdom) — closer to the classical Prudence emblem [@web_steve_grandet]. 

*Sources:* [@web_steve_grandet].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md).
sections.Scene: La Prudence in the Egyptian Etteilla shows wisdom and foresight in an Egyptian context — perhaps with the eye of Horus, the was-sceptre of divine authority, or the double-headed uraeus serpent. Egyptian symbolic wisdom is remarkably consonant with the classical iconography of Prudence, both emphasizing the serpent as emblem of far-sighted knowledge.
sections.Symbol: Prudence in Egyptian form — the eye of Horus or the uraeus serpent as wisdom symbol, foresight rendered through Egyptian divine iconography.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Grand Prêtre — The High Priest
sections.Upright: Counsel, sanction, marriage, spiritual authority; a trusted guide.
sections.Reversed: Hollow dogma, a bad counsellor, hypocrisy in high place.
sections.Correction: Correction (2026-06-13, research/cards/<deck>.md): mapped to the Tarot de Marseille trump this card actually reworks; the 'priest'/'despot' titles are Etteilla-II/III re-labels. Sources: etteillastrumps, benebell wen, steve-p.org. (Card 13 = the Lovers / 'Le Mariage'.)
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **re-titled, not re-identified.** Etteilla II/III print the card as **"Le Grand Prêtre"** — naming the **officiant** who solemnises the marriage rather than the union itself. This is why modern keys (and the repo grammar) attach it to the **Hierophant**; but the card's lineage is the **Lovers → Etteilla's Marriage**, and the High-Priest reading is a **posthumous title drift**, shared with Etteilla III [@web_steve_grandet; @web_druidcraft; @web_etteillastrumps]. **— This is the title that misleads the grammar's `arcana:the-hierophant` mapping (see corrections).**. 

*Sources:* [@web_druidcraft], [@web_etteilla_org], [@web_etteillastrumps], [@web_mccloskys_lismon], [@web_steve_grandet].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md).
sections.Scene: Le Grand Prêtre in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts the High Priest as an Egyptian high priest or hierophant — perhaps with the double crown, the was-sceptre, and the vestments of the temple priesthood. The Egyptian High Priest sits at the center of the civilization's sacred knowledge transmission, an exact equivalent of the Hierophant in the Egyptian context.
sections.Symbol: The High Priest in Egyptian form — the keeper of temple wisdom in the Egyptian priestly tradition, sacred authority in the language of the Nile civilization.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Diable — Force / The Devil
sections.Upright: Raw force and vehemence — power over circumstance (Etteilla read the Devil as energy, not only evil).
sections.Reversed: Fury and ravage; bondage to one's own passions.
sections.Scene: Le Diable in the Egyptian Etteilla shows infernal or underworld forces in an Egyptian idiom — perhaps with Set (the Egyptian god of chaos and destruction) or underworld demons from the Amduat. The Egyptian concept of isfet (chaos) provides the equivalent of the Western Devil in this Egyptomanie context.
sections.Symbol: The Devil in Egyptian form — perhaps Set or the demons of the underworld, chaos and destructive force rendered through Egyptian cosmological myth.

items.name: Le Magicien ou le Bateleur (The Magician/Juggler)

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Magicien / Le Bateleur — Illness
sections.Upright: The juggler's hand at work; in Etteilla's system the Bateleur governs illness and its cure — a matter of the body, skill applied.
sections.Reversed: A charlatan, trickery; malady worsening, a false remedy.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** **image redrawn.** The magician now stands to the **right** of the table (was the left), with a small **statue (some read a homunculus)** added on the table before him [@web_steve_grandet]. 

*Sources:* [@web_steve_grandet].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/etteilla-ii-egyptian.md).
sections.Scene: Le Magicien in the Egyptian Etteilla shows the conjurer or skilled practitioner in an Egyptian context — perhaps as Thoth with his writing implements, or as an Egyptian scribe or magician with papyrus scrolls and magical instruments. The Egyptian tradition of heka (magic) makes this card especially resonant in the Egyptomanie idiom.
sections.Symbol: The Magician in Egyptian form — perhaps Thoth with papyrus and stylus, skilled mastery expressed through the Egyptian tradition of sacred magic and writing.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Jugement Dernier — Last Judgement
sections.Upright: Awakening, a decisive change, renewal answering the trumpet's call.
sections.Reversed: A verdict postponed; fear of change, the summons unheeded.
sections.Scene: Le Jugement Dernier in the Egyptian Etteilla depicts divine judgment in an Egyptian context — perhaps as the weighing of the heart in the Hall of Ma'at, with Osiris presiding, Anubis at the scales, and Thoth recording. The Egyptian afterlife judgment is among the most developed in the ancient world and provides a precise iconographic equivalent for this card.
sections.Symbol: Judgment in Egyptian form — the weighing of the heart before Osiris, the divine reckoning expressed through the Egyptian afterlife tribunal.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Mort — Death
sections.Upright: Transformation, the necessary end that clears the ground.
sections.Reversed: Inertia; a change resisted, an ending only half-made.
sections.Scene: La Mort in the Egyptian Etteilla shows Death in an Egyptian idiom — perhaps as Osiris (the god of the dead and resurrection), or as the mummification scene, or as the divine jackal Anubis leading souls to judgment. Death in Egyptian thought is the threshold to the afterlife, making this card resonant with transformation rather than mere ending.
sections.Symbol: Death in Egyptian form — perhaps Osiris or Anubis, mortality expressed through the Egyptian tradition of death as threshold and transformation.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Capucin — The Traitor / Hermit
sections.Upright: The hooded solitary; in Etteilla's reading the Capucin is the traitor — concealment, a matter hidden, or prudent withdrawal.
sections.Reversed: A secret enemy unmasked; treachery, false counsel exposed.
sections.Scene: Le Capucin in the Egyptian Etteilla replaces the Christian monk with an Egyptian ascetic or priestly figure — perhaps a desert hermit, a prophet on the margins of the Nile civilization, or an Egyptian temple recluse. The withdrawal from society is reframed in Egyptian terms as the solitary wisdom of the desert.
sections.Symbol: The Hermit in Egyptian form — the desert ascetic or temple recluse, withdrawal and solitude expressed through the Egyptian tradition of the contemplative.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Temple Foudroyé — The Struck Temple
sections.Upright: The temple thrown down: sudden ruin, downfall, a structure broken open.
sections.Reversed: A calamity narrowly escaped — but oppression, confinement, ruin deferred.
sections.Scene: Le Temple Foudroyé in the Egyptian Etteilla shows a struck or ruined Egyptian temple — perhaps an obelisk struck by lightning, a pylon shattered by divine force, or a pyramid in collapse. The destruction of Egyptian sacred architecture carries a particular weight given the Egyptomanie context: the ruin of the most enduring civilization's monuments as emblem of all things' passing.
sections.Symbol: The Struck Egyptian Temple — obelisk or pylon shattered by celestial force, the ruin of the most enduring sacred architecture as emblem of all things' passing.

items.name: La Roue de Fortune (Wheel of Fortune)

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Roue de Fortune — Wheel of Fortune
sections.Upright: Fortune turning upward: increase, elevation, luck in the matter.
sections.Reversed: A fall; fortune reversed, gain turning to loss.
sections.Scene: La Roue de Fortune in the Egyptian Etteilla shows the Wheel of Fortune in an Egyptian context — perhaps with Egyptian figures ascending and descending, or with the wheel replaced by a solar cycle or the Nile's annual flood cycle. Fortune's turning is expressed through the cyclical rhythms of the Egyptian world.
sections.Symbol: The Wheel of Fortune in Egyptian form — the cycle of the Nile or the solar cycle as Fortune's turning, cyclical rise and fall in the language of Egyptian temporal rhythm.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: Le Despote Africain — The Sovereign
sections.Upright: Great power and a powerful protector; dissension mastered by a strong hand.
sections.Reversed: Tyranny; a dangerous man, discord stirred from above.

sections.Scene: Le Despote Africain in the Egyptian Etteilla — the African Despot is perhaps the most naturally placed card in this Egyptian version. The card shows a pharaoh or African sovereign at the apex of the world, a crowned ruler whose authority is rendered in full Egyptian royal iconography: double crown, crook and flail, divine regalia. This is perhaps the card where Etteilla II is most iconographically at home.
sections.Symbol: The Pharaoh-King at the apex — the African sovereign in full Egyptian royal regalia, the despot of Etteilla's revised sequence shown as the crowned pharaoh of the Nile civilization.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Bâtons (Batons) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (enterprise, work, the countryside and growth). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): An enterprising, honourable man — of the country or of business; a good friend in practical affairs.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): The same man turned harsh, grasping, or unreliable in his dealings.
sections.Scene: Roi de Bâton (King of Wands) shows a crowned or enthroned male figure associated with the wand suit — a king who embodies the fiery, creative, and enterprising qualities of the baton. In the Etteilla system the King of Wands is associated with creative authority and entrepreneurial leadership, a figure who commands through vision and enthusiasm.
sections.Symbol: King of Wands — creative authority and entrepreneurial leadership, the king who commands through vision and passionate enterprise.

sections.Scene: Huit d'Épée (Eight of Swords) shows eight sword pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The eight of swords in the Etteilla system is associated with restriction and confusion — the binding of the mind by its own constructions, the feeling of being trapped by thought.
sections.Symbol: Eight Swords — restriction and mental confusion, the mind bound by its own constructions.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Seven of Épées (Swords) — the suit of Air, governing conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble.
sections.Upright: a hope and a partial success, still uncertain, within conflict.
sections.Reversed: The hope dashed; effort abandoned.
sections.Scene: Sept d'Épée (Seven of Swords) shows seven sword pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The seven of swords in the Etteilla system is associated with strategy, theft, or the evasive maneuver — the suit's intelligence applied to circumventing opposition rather than confronting it directly.
sections.Symbol: Seven Swords — strategy and evasion, intelligence applied to circumventing obstacles rather than confronting them directly.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Six of Épées (Swords) — the suit of Air, governing conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble.
sections.Upright: a passage, a road, fair give-and-take, within conflict.
sections.Reversed: The way blocked; an exchange gone unequal.
sections.Scene: Six d'Épée (Six of Swords) shows six sword pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The six of swords in the Etteilla system is associated with transition and passage — moving away from turbulent waters toward calmer conditions, the journey of recovery from mental turmoil.
sections.Symbol: Six Swords — transition toward calmer conditions, the recovery journey away from mental turmoil.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Five of Épées (Swords) — the suit of Air, governing conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble.
sections.Upright: a turn or loss within the matter — a test, within conflict.
sections.Reversed: The loss easing, or the test deepening.
sections.Scene: Cinq d'Épée (Five of Swords) shows five sword pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The five of swords in the Etteilla system is associated with defeat and hollow victory — the pyrrhic win that comes at too great a cost, conflict that damages all parties.
sections.Symbol: Five Swords — defeat or hollow victory, conflict that damages all parties regardless of its outcome.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Four of Épées (Swords) — the suit of Air, governing conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble.
sections.Upright: a settling, a small security, a needed pause, within conflict.
sections.Reversed: Stagnation; holding on too tightly.
sections.Scene: Quatre d'Épée (Four of Swords) shows four sword pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The four of swords in the Etteilla system is associated with rest and recuperation — the knight's effigy on a tomb, rest as preparation for renewed action after a period of mental or physical exhaustion.
sections.Symbol: Four Swords — rest and recuperation, the strategic withdrawal from activity to restore mental and physical reserves.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Three of Épées (Swords) — the suit of Air, governing conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble.
sections.Upright: increase; a third party, growth, the thing taking shape, within conflict.
sections.Reversed: Growth checked; effort scattered, a delay.
sections.Scene: Trois d'Épée (Three of Swords) shows three sword pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The three of swords in the Etteilla system is associated with heartbreak and sorrow — the precise moment of loss or separation that leaves a wound in the emotional life.
sections.Symbol: Three Swords — heartbreak and sorrow, the precise wound of loss or separation.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Two of Épées (Swords) — the suit of Air, governing conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble.
sections.Upright: a meeting or exchange; the matter shared with another, within conflict.
sections.Reversed: Division, a pairing strained or broken.
sections.Scene: Deux d'Épée (Two of Swords) shows two sword pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The two of swords in the Etteilla system is associated with stalemate and willful blindness — two blades crossed in a truce that is actually an avoidance of the truth.
sections.Symbol: Two Swords — stalemate and willful blindness, the truce that avoids facing the truth of a difficult situation.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Ace of Épées (Swords) — the suit of Air, governing conflict, law, arms and sharp trouble.
sections.Upright: a strong onset of the matter — the suit's force at its purest, within conflict.
sections.Reversed: That force blocked or misdirected at the very start.
sections.Scene: As d'Épée (Ace of Swords) shows a single sword as the suit's foundational emblem. In the Etteilla system the Ace of Swords is the undivided force of mental clarity — the blade of truth before it is used for either justice or cruelty, the intellectual potential in its most concentrated form.
sections.Symbol: Ace of Swords — undivided mental clarity, the blade of truth in its most concentrated and generative form.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Deniers (Coins) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (money, trade, property and practical gain). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A man of means — a merchant or man of property; reliable in money, a useful protector.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A miserly or corrupt man; wealth used coldly, gain without scruple.
sections.Scene: Roi de Denier (King of Coins) shows a crowned or enthroned male figure associated with the coin suit — a king of practical authority, material success, and the stable governance of earthly resources. In the Etteilla system the King of Coins is a figure of financial and material authority, one who commands through demonstrated competence and reliable prosperity.
sections.Symbol: King of Coins — practical authority and material success, the king who commands through demonstrated competence and reliable prosperity.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Deniers (Coins) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (money, trade, property and practical gain). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A prosperous, practical woman; security, a generous and capable manager.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A grasping or showy woman; luxury without warmth.
sections.Scene: Reine de Denier (Queen of Coins) shows a crowned female figure associated with the coin suit — a queen of practical nurturing, material generosity, and the intelligent management of earthly resources. In the Etteilla system the Queen of Coins is a figure of domestic authority and material warmth, one who makes the household flourish through careful stewardship.
sections.Symbol: Queen of Coins — practical nurturing and material generosity, the queen who makes resources flourish through careful stewardship.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Deniers (Coins) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (money, trade, property and practical gain). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): Money arriving, a useful and dependable man; a profitable matter approaching.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): A stalled payment, a heavy and unimaginative man; gain delayed.
sections.Scene: Chevalier de Denier (Knight of Coins) shows a mounted figure bearing a coin — the suit's active rank carrying material ambition into patient, methodical pursuit. The Knight of Coins in the Etteilla system is the most deliberate and careful of the suit's court figures, his progress slow but reliable.
sections.Symbol: Knight of Coins — patient and methodical material pursuit, the reliable plodder who reaches his goal through steady effort rather than swift charge.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: A court of Deniers (Coins) — and so a PERSON. In Etteilla's cartomancy every court card signifies someone in the querent's life, coloured by the suit (money, trade, property and practical gain). Upright the person is well-disposed; reversed, ill-disposed.
sections.Upright (the person well-disposed): A student or clerk, news of money or study; diligence, a small material beginning.
sections.Reversed (the person ill-disposed): Bad news of money, a careless clerk; effort that earns little.
sections.Scene: Valet de Denier (Page of Coins) shows a youthful figure studying a coin — the suit's youngest court rank, a diligent student of practical matters and material skills. The Page of Coins in the Etteilla system is the earnest learner of practical competencies, a young person focused on developing real-world skills.
sections.Symbol: Page of Coins — diligent study of practical skills, the earnest young student of material competence and earthly know-how.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Ten of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: the matter at its fullest — completion, or surfeit, within money.
sections.Reversed: Excess and burden; a cycle that overstays.
sections.Scene: Dix de Denier (Ten of Coins) shows ten coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The ten of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with material completion and generational wealth — the abundance that extends beyond the individual to the family and community.
sections.Symbol: Ten Coins — material completion and generational wealth, abundance that extends to the family and the community.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Nine of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: near-fulfilment; intensity, the thing almost complete, within money.
sections.Reversed: Strain, delay, intensity turned against itself.
sections.Scene: Neuf de Denier (Nine of Coins) shows nine coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The nine of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with material comfort and refined self-sufficiency — the enjoyment of earned prosperity in a cultivated environment.
sections.Symbol: Nine Coins — material comfort and refined self-sufficiency, the enjoyment of earned prosperity.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Eight of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: comings and goings, change, the matter in motion, within money.
sections.Reversed: Movement stalled; restlessness without progress.
sections.Scene: Huit de Denier (Eight of Coins) shows eight coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The eight of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with skill development and craftsmanship — the diligent repetitive practice that builds material competence.
sections.Symbol: Eight Coins — skill development and craftsmanship, the diligent practice that builds material competence.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Seven of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: a hope and a partial success, still uncertain, within money.
sections.Reversed: The hope dashed; effort abandoned.
sections.Scene: Sept de Denier (Seven of Coins) shows seven coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The seven of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with patient waiting for material results — the farmer who has planted and now waits to harvest, the assessment of what has been invested.
sections.Symbol: Seven Coins — patient assessment and waiting for results, the farmer's pause between planting and harvest.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Six of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: a passage, a road, fair give-and-take, within money.
sections.Reversed: The way blocked; an exchange gone unequal.
sections.Scene: Six de Denier (Six of Coins) shows six coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The six of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with generosity and equitable distribution — the giving and receiving of material resources in balanced exchange.
sections.Symbol: Six Coins — generosity and equitable distribution, material resources given and received in balanced exchange.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Five of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: a turn or loss within the matter — a test, within money.
sections.Reversed: The loss easing, or the test deepening.
sections.Scene: Cinq de Denier (Five of Coins) shows five coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The five of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with material hardship and exclusion — the experience of being cold and without support, material or spiritual poverty.
sections.Symbol: Five Coins — material hardship and exclusion, the experience of poverty or cold exclusion from available resources.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Four of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: a settling, a small security, a needed pause, within money.
sections.Reversed: Stagnation; holding on too tightly.
sections.Scene: Quatre de Denier (Four of Coins) shows four coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The four of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with material security and possessiveness — the stable holding of resources that risks becoming rigid attachment.
sections.Symbol: Four Coins — material security and possessiveness, the stable holding of resources that may become rigid attachment.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Three of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: increase; a third party, growth, the thing taking shape, within money.
sections.Reversed: Growth checked; effort scattered, a delay.
sections.Scene: Trois de Denier (Three of Coins) shows three coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The three of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with skilled work and collaborative achievement — the master craftsman at work, material competence applied in a context of recognized expertise.
sections.Symbol: Three Coins — skilled work and collaborative achievement, the master craftsman whose expertise is recognized.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Two of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: a meeting or exchange; the matter shared with another, within money.
sections.Reversed: Division, a pairing strained or broken.
sections.Scene: Deux de Denier (Two of Coins) shows two coin pip symbols with divinatory keywords. The two of coins in the Etteilla system is associated with balance and juggling — the management of multiple material responsibilities simultaneously, adaptability in the face of fluctuating circumstances.
sections.Symbol: Two Coins — balance and juggling, the management of multiple material responsibilities in adaptable equilibrium.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: The Ace of Deniers (Coins) — the suit of Earth, governing money, trade, property and practical gain.
sections.Upright: a strong onset of the matter — the suit's force at its purest, within money.
sections.Reversed: That force blocked or misdirected at the very start.
sections.Scene: As de Denier (Ace of Coins) shows a single coin as the suit's foundational emblem. In the Etteilla system the Ace of Coins is the undivided potential of material manifestation — the seed of prosperity, the opportunity for practical achievement in its most concentrated form.
sections.Symbol: Ace of Coins — the undivided material potential, the seed of prosperity and the concentrated opportunity for practical achievement.

sections.Card in Etteilla's System: La Folie — the female querent (significator)
sections.Upright: The female consultant herself, and the wild card outside the count: the matter unbound, open to any turn.
sections.Reversed: Recklessness; a question asked in folly, energy with no rein.
sections.Correction: Correction (2026-06-13): La Folie is the Fool (0), not the female querent (that is Card 8, Le Repos).
sections.Scene: La Folie in the Egyptian Etteilla shows the Fool in an Egyptian or exotic costume, perhaps as a wandering figure with Egyptian attire, or as a court jester from an orientalist imaginary. The Fool placed at card 78 in the Egyptomanie idiom wanders outside the structured world of the pharaoh's court.
sections.Symbol: The Fool in Egyptian context — the wandering figure at the margins of the ancient world's most ordered civilization, folly at number 78 in the Egyptomanie sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “balance” (6).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with the divinatory theme of Balance in the Etteilla system — the quality of equilibrium between opposing forces, the measure of just proportion. Balance in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Cardinal Virtue of Justice, with the two-cup Temperance, and with pip cards in the middle range of the suits.
sections.Symbol: Balance — the equilibrium between opposing forces, justice and measure as the achieved state of the consulting oracle.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “creativity” (5).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Creativity in the Etteilla system — the generative impulse, the power of imaginative invention and artistic expression. Creativity in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Wands suit and with the early Genesis cards' depiction of the creative acts of divine order.
sections.Symbol: Creativity — the generative impulse of invention and artistic expression, associated with the suit of fire and the creative acts of the Genesis sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “truth” (5).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Truth in the Etteilla system — the quality of accurate perception and honest expression. Truth in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Cardinal Virtues, with the Swords suit's cutting clarity, and with the oracle's fundamental purpose of revealing what is actually the case.
sections.Symbol: Truth — accurate perception and honest expression, the fundamental aim of the Etteilla oracle.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “patience” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Patience in the Etteilla system — the quality of waiting, enduring, and allowing things to unfold in their proper time. Patience in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the slower Coin suit cards and with the Genesis sequence's depiction of creation as an ordered temporal process.
sections.Symbol: Patience — the quality of enduring and allowing things to unfold, associated with the methodical suit of coins and the temporal ordering of creation.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “security” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Security in the Etteilla system — the quality of stable foundation, material safety, and protection from threat. Security in the Etteilla tradition is associated with stable pip cards in the Coin suit and with figures of established authority.
sections.Symbol: Security — stable foundation and material safety, associated with the Coin suit's establishment of reliable earthly resources.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “wisdom” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Wisdom in the Etteilla system — deep understanding, the knowledge that comes from experience rather than from mere information. Wisdom in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Prudence trump, with senior court figures, and with the accumulated insight of the oracle itself.
sections.Symbol: Wisdom — deep understanding forged from experience, the mature knowledge that the oracle channels for the questioner.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “abundance” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Abundance in the Etteilla system — the fullness of material, emotional, or creative resources. Abundance in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the ten-pip cards and the higher pip counts in each suit.
sections.Symbol: Abundance — the fullness of available resources, associated with the high-count pip cards where each suit reaches its maximum expression.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “clarity” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Clarity in the Etteilla system — the quality of clear perception, unobstructed understanding, and the removal of confusion or illusion. Clarity in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Swords suit's mental sharpness and with the oracle's function of illuminating what is obscure.
sections.Symbol: Clarity — unobstructed understanding and clear perception, the quality the oracle provides by cutting through confusion.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “guidance” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Guidance in the Etteilla system — the direction provided by the oracle, by authority figures, or by inner wisdom. Guidance in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the High Priest and other figures of spiritual and social authority, as well as with the oracle itself as a tool for navigating uncertain situations.
sections.Symbol: Guidance — the direction provided by authority or inner wisdom, the fundamental service of the Etteilla oracle to the questioner.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “harmony” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Harmony in the Etteilla system — the quality of agreement, peace, and the concordance of disparate elements into a unified whole. Harmony in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the stable mid-range pip cards and with positive resolutions in the reading.
sections.Symbol: Harmony — the agreement and concordance of disparate elements, associated with stable and positive outcomes in the Etteilla reading.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “intuition” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Intuition in the Etteilla system — the direct knowing that bypasses conscious reasoning, the inner voice or felt sense of what is true. Intuition in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Cups suit's emotional intelligence and with the oracle reading itself as an act of non-rational perception.
sections.Symbol: Intuition — direct knowing beyond reasoning, associated with the Cups suit's emotional intelligence and with the oracular process itself.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “manifestation” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Manifestation in the Etteilla system — the process by which inner intentions become outer realities. Manifestation in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the early Genesis cards' depiction of creation and with the Coin suit's domain of practical material results.
sections.Symbol: Manifestation — the process of bringing inner intention into material form, associated with the creative Genesis sequence and the practical Coin suit.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “nurturing” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Nurturing in the Etteilla system — the quality of caring support, the tending of what is growing or vulnerable. Nurturing in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Queen figures across the suits and with the Cups suit's emotional care.
sections.Symbol: Nurturing — caring support for what is growing or vulnerable, associated with the Queen courts and the Cups suit's emotional care.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “planning” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Planning in the Etteilla system — the capacity for forethought, preparation, and the systematic organization of resources toward a goal. Planning in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Prudence trump and with the methodical Coin suit's patient accumulation.
sections.Symbol: Planning — forethought and systematic preparation, associated with the Prudence trump and the Coin suit's patient organization of resources.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “skill” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping items associated with Skill in the Etteilla system — the developed competence in a particular domain, the mastery acquired through practice. Skill in the Etteilla tradition is associated with the Magician trump and with the craftsman-related pip cards in the Coin and Wands suits.
sections.Symbol: Skill — developed competence through practice, associated with the Magician's mastery and the craftsman pip cards across the suits.

sections.Description: Cross-cutting facets derived from shared keywords — the same card appears under every keyword it carries.
sections.Scene: A navigational axis organizing all items in the Etteilla deck by keyword — grouping the cards by their associated divinatory themes and qualities. The keyword axis allows navigation across the deck through themes like balance, creativity, wisdom, and manifestation rather than through suit or trump sequence.
sections.Symbol: The keyword axis — a thematic cross-index of the Etteilla oracle, allowing approach to its 94 items through the qualities and divinatory themes that connect cards across suits and trumps.
